description_long: This object is a traditional Japanese torii gate, typically found at the entrance to Shinto shrines. It features two vertical pillars supporting two horizontal crossbars, with the upper crossbar slightly curved upward at both ends, giving it a distinctive silhouette. The structure is simple and elegant, with clean lines and minimal ornamentation, emphasizing its spiritual and cultural significance. The pillars are cylindrical and robust, anchored with rounded bases, while the crossbars are rectangular in profile. The overall color is a dark, muted tone, suggesting it may be constructed from painted wood or metal. This gate serves as a symbolic threshold between the mundane and the sacred, inviting visitors to pass through.
description: A traditional Japanese torii gate with two vertical pillars and two horizontal crossbars, the upper one gracefully curved. The structure is dark-colored, minimalistic, and culturally significant.
description_view_0: Front view showing both vertical pillars, the curved upper crossbar, and the full width of the gate.
description_view_1: Side view revealing the thin profile of the gate and the curvature of the top crossbar.
description_view_2: Rear view, visually identical to the front, displaying both pillars and the crossbars.
description_view_3: Opposite side view, again showing the slim profile and the upward curve of the top crossbar.
